summary: This is a brief link-blog entry where Joel Spolsky shares two items of interest. First, he highlights Eric Sink's analysis of leaky abstractions encountered while building Vault, a source control system in .NET, where Sink favorably compares .NET to Java's reliability in later stages of development. Second, Joel mentions William Poundstone's new book about Microsoft's interview practices and puzzle questions.
key_points: Eric Sink found that Java's productivity gains were offset by bugs caused by leaky abstractions late in the development cycle
.NET is presented as a more reliable platform that avoided the abstraction-related failures Sink experienced with Java
Joel continues his interest in the topic of leaky abstractions, a concept he popularized in his own writing
William Poundstone's book on Microsoft interview puzzles touches on hiring practices Joel has written about extensively
money_quotes: I've used Java extensively, and I loved the productivity gains we got during the beginning and middle of the development cycle. But things got ugly at the end.
All those layers of abstraction started contributing to our bug list.
I've been involved in a couple of projects which completely failed because Java was chosen.
This success stands as a testimony to how incredible .NET really is.
key_insight: Platform abstractions that boost early productivity can become liabilities late in development when their leaks manifest as hard-to-fix bugs.
